video_title: Top #DDD Videos in the Bay Area with Guy Fieri | Diners, Drive-Ins and Dives | Food Network

restaurants.name: Señor Sisig
restaurants.guy_intro: San Francisco in the Soma District, south of Market, to look for a food truck. Now, I hear these guys are doing a cross between Filipino and Latin American cuisine. You never know where they're gonna be, so you gotta check them out on their website. And I found them. This is Señor Sisig.

dishes.description: Pork shoulder marinated overnight in soy sauce, sugarcane vinegar, pineapple juice, garlic, bay leaf, sugar, salt, and pepper. Char-boiled, chopped, then mixed with a final marinade of soy sauce, lemon juice, red chili flakes, and black pepper. Reheated on a flat top and served on warm corn tortillas with diced onions, jalapeno, iceberg lettuce, cabbage, cilantro cream sauce, and a squeeze of lime.
dishes.guy_response: That pork has got so much flavor. Mm. Salty, spicy goodness. Yeah. I've had a lot of $4 tacos, not one with that much meat in it. Mm. That's packed with big flavor. Nice job. Right on. Nice job.

dishes.description: Chicken drumettes and wings marinated for five hours in soy sauce, sugarcane vinegar, garlic, bay leaves, crushed peppercorn, and salt. Hard-baked, then deep-fried. Glazed with a sauce made from olive oil, garlic, peppercorn, bay leaves, soy sauce, sugarcane vinegar, chipotle peppers, ketchup, honey, brown sugar, cayenne pepper, red chili flakes, Worcestershire sauce, and salt, thickened with a cornstarch slurry. Served on shrimp chips with sesame seeds and green onion lemon zest garnish.
dishes.guy_response: I can't figure out the flavor. All of a sudden I get soy sauce. Now I get chipotle. Now I get a little bit of the garlic. I think it's sweet. Yeah. The wings are cooked perfectly. Little big wings. Great crust to them and the shrimp chips as accompaniment. Right on. For sure. Not too sweet, not too spicy, not Asian, not Mexican. Just senior seasick. Yeah. Nicely done my friend.

restaurants.name: The Front Porch
restaurants.guy_intro: Well, here I am in the southeast side of the Mission District to check out a joint that started in the 30s as an Italian restaurant in a garage. Well, fast forward to 2006, and now it's a funky little joint that's serving up some killer southern comfort food. Oh, that's right. You got to come kick it at the front porch.

dishes.description: Creamy, cheesy grits made with whole milk, water, unsalted butter, stone-ground grits, black pepper, kosher salt, and sharp cheddar cheese. Served with a spicy red-eye gravy featuring canola oil, shallots, garlic, andouille sausage, Creole spice (kosher salt, black pepper, white pepper, cayenne pepper, paprika, dark chili powder, coriander, cumin, ground mustard), Worcestershire sauce, Tabasco, chicory coffee, and chicken stock. Wild gulf shrimp are poached in the gravy with more Creole spice and butter, then served over the grits, topped with the reduced gravy and green onions.
dishes.guy_response: This isn't exactly how I know red-eye gravy to be where I'm from. But I will tell you, it is awesome. Got a little coffee kick. I mean, it's rich. Grits are cheesy. The andouille is spot on. Everything going on in this is outstanding. But really, the crescendo is those fresh shrimp. I mean, succulent. Have I ever used that word on Triple D? Oh, my gosh. Killer dish. Cool.

restaurants.name: Radio Africa in Kitchen
restaurants.guy_intro: So I'm here on the southeast side of San Francisco in an area known as the Bayview District, here to check out a chef that's competed twice on Triple D. I was so impressed with how he competed, I have to check out his restaurant. This is Radio Africa in Kitchen.

dishes.description: Deboned chicken thighs and tenderloins marinated in salt, pepper, fresh thyme, fresh oregano, lemon juice, pomegranate, and olive oil. The thighs are grilled, and the tenderloins are cooked separately. A house-made berbere mix (bird's eye chili, granulated garlic, granulated onion, cinnamon, cardamom, Ethiopian basil, black pepper, salt) is added to roasted and chopped tomatoes. This mixture is combined with basmati and brown wild rice, sea salt, kale, sausage, and spinach.
dishes.guy_response: The depth of flavor, it's very inviting. It's not spicy. The greens are nice and vibrant. The chicken is tender. The veggies are dynamite. The berbere, that little mix, oh, spot on. Mm. Is your chicken tambalaya? It's the juiciest, most succulent chicken I've ever had.

restaurants.name: Rosine's
restaurants.guy_intro: So I'm here in Monterey, California, known for Cannery Row and awesome seafood. Well, I'm in the locals area, I'm downtown, to check out a joint that the locals love, an old-school, family-run Italian joint. This is Rosie.

dishes.description: A unique minestrone starting cold with top round beef, dry red beans, granulated garlic and onions, sugar, oregano, sweet basil, black pepper, bay leaves, white pepper, tomato paste, and beef base, cooked with water, diced tomatoes, and tomato sauce for four hours. The beef is then pulled out and shredded. Celery, carrots, zucchini, onions, and cabbage are added and cooked for another hour until tender. The shredded beef is added back, and the soup is served with pasta and Parmesan cheese.
dishes.guy_response: I love the cabbage in there, the shredded beef, not something you typically find in minestrone. A nice rich broth. I mean, the whole thing. Great. It's delicious. But I want to call the real boss in. Rosine, are you back there? Now this is the authority. You got a lot to be proud of, girl. Very nice to meet you. Thank you, Guy. Very nice to meet you. And look at her. I mean, to the nines. The hair, the bling. We have a rule that there is no... You can't wear more bling than the host of the show. Oh, my gosh. Minestrone. Can't wait to get in your mouth. As soon as you taste it, you know that it's absolutely homemade.

dishes.description: Boneless beef short ribs seasoned with salt and black pepper, then seared in canola oil. Braised with mirepoix (carrots, celery, onion), fresh thyme, bay leaves, chipotle peppers in adobo sauce, homemade chicken stock, and a generous amount of Dr. Pepper. Cooked in the oven at 350 degrees for four hours. Served with celery root puree, sauteed mustard greens, and the rich, reduced Dr. Pepper braising liquid, garnished with fresh grated horseradish.
dishes.guy_response: Very good and you should probably put it all in the trunk of my car and I'll get rid of it for you. The short rib is so tender that it's just falling apart. You just have to help it with the fork a little bit. The celery root puree is a great medium, the whole dish. The fresh horseradish, along with the mustard greens, adds a nice contrast to this braising liquid that's been reduced. And the Dr. Pepper, which is this nice round flavor and... Mm. Every little piece you did here, killer. Awesome. Thank you. I mean, really killer. Cool.

restaurants.name: The Refuge
restaurants.guy_intro: I love food. I know it's pretty evident, but there's particular types of food that I really dig and pastrami is one of them. I'm talking about old-school, real deal pastrami. The kind of pastrami you get on the East Coast when you go to Katz's Deli in the Lower East Side of Manhattan. So if you're on the West Coast, I hear you got to go to San Carlos, California. Right between San Francisco and San Jose. And check out this joint, The Refuge.

dishes.description: Pastrami brined for a week, then seasoned with coriander and black pepper, smoked with hickory, and steamed for four hours until tender. The drippings are reduced with brown sugar, black pepper, and coriander to create a lacquer. The meat is trimmed, dipped in the lacquer, and then sliced. Served on toasted rye bread with Russian dressing, coleslaw, and Swiss cheese.
dishes.guy_response: Yeah, this is ridiculous. You can be a hardcore New Yorker, nothing's gonna prepare you for that. It's that good, man. Really nice toasted rye. Perfectly brined, smoked, steamed. It's that good. I'm not kidding you. Night-night.

restaurants.name: La Texanita
restaurants.guy_intro: You know, everybody's got their hometown favorites. Here in our hometown of Santa Rosa, California, Hunter and I have been rolling to this place for years. Now, there's all kinds of great Mexican restaurants in this area, but for some reason, between the story, the people, and the great food, La Texanita has become one of our favorites.

dishes.description: Handmade tortillas crafted from dried corn masa. Carne asada is cooked with chopped onions and finished with a squeeze of lime juice. Served on the fresh tortillas with onions, cilantro, house-made salsa, radishes, and jalapenos.
dishes.guy_response: So simple but so good. Onions almost have their own sweetness to them and the natural juices and the fat from the meat is a whole lot to it. Here's the tortilla. Cooked everything, but I'm telling you something. There's just nothing that can beat the hand made tortilla. I'm cooking it the right way. Shake your booty. And shake your booty the right way. Great job. The crunch from the onion, the softness of the tortilla, just heaven in your mouth. The meat's what makes it, there's just a delicate taste to it. It's the most authentic taco truck carne asada taco I could possibly get.

dishes.description: A huarache, which is a thick, oval-shaped tortilla, with a layer of beans in between, topped with tender carnitas and a drizzle of cream.
dishes.guy_response: Great texture of the carnitas, thin layer of beans. Fantastic dish. Very graceful.
